lesson.menu_desc: Phase 2 -- modal perfects (should have, could have, might have) for past speculation -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 13 -- What Could Have Happened -- Modal Perfects
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Mark Kazuyoshi Seki Omagari | Aula 13 -- What Could Have Happened

listenings.text: Hi Mark. Last class you talked about wishes and regrets. Today we go one step further: we look back at the past and imagine a different outcome. For this, English uses modal perfects: a modal verb plus have plus the past participle. Here is the key. Use should have plus the past participle to talk about the right thing to do that you did not do, like I should have studied more. Use could have to talk about a possibility that existed but did not happen, like I could have taken the earlier train. Use might have or may have when you are not sure, when you only guess about the past, like She might have missed the bus. And use would have for the imagined result of a different choice, like I would have passed if I had tried. Notice that in fast speech, should have often sounds like should've, and would have sounds like would've. The whole idea is to look back and judge or guess what was possible. So think about yesterday. What should you have done? What could have happened if you had chosen differently? See you in class, Mark.

Let me look back at something that happened to me last week. I had a math test, and honestly, the outcome was not great. In hindsight, I should have started studying earlier instead of leaving everything for the last night. I could have asked my teacher for help, but I hesitated, and that was a reckless choice. The plan to study at midnight backfired completely, because I was too tired to focus. If I had been more cautious, I might have done much better. I keep thinking I would have passed easily if I had spared just one hour a day during the week. It was a hard lesson, but a worthwhile one. Now I know that good preparation is bound to pay off, and that pulling off a good grade at the last minute is almost never inevitable. Next time, I will not overlook the basics.

extra_audio.text: First, you decide what you want to say about the past: a criticism, a missed chance, a guess, or an imagined result. Then, for the right thing you did not do, you use should have plus the past participle. Next, for a chance that existed but did not happen, you use could have. After that, for a guess when you are not sure, you use might have or may have. Finally, for the imagined result of a different choice, you use would have, often with an if clause.
